concepts.name: Structure of Amines
concepts.explanation: Amines are derivatives of ammonia where one or more hydrogen atoms are replaced by alkyl/aryl groups. The nitrogen atom is spآ³ hybridised with a pyramidal geometry and an unshared pair of electrons. The C-N-E bond angle is less than 109.5آ° (e.g., 108آ° in trimethylamine) due to lone pair repulsion.

concepts.name: Classification of Amines â€“ Primary, Secondary, Tertiary
concepts.explanation: Amines are classified based on the number of hydrogen atoms replaced in ammonia: primary (1آ° â€“ RNHâ‚‚), secondary (2آ° â€“ Râ‚‚NH), and tertiary (3آ° â€“ Râ‚ƒN). Aryl amines have the -NHâ‚‚ group directly attached to an aromatic ring (e.g., aniline, Câ‚†Hâ‚…NHâ‚‚).

concepts.name: Nomenclature of Amines â€“ Common and IUPAC
concepts.explanation: Common names: prefix alkyl groups to 'amine' (e.g., methylamine). IUPAC: primary amines are named as alkanamines (e.g., methanamine for CHâ‚ƒNHâ‚‚). Secondary and tertiary amines use N locants (e.g., N-methylethanamine, N,N-dimethylethanamine). Arylamines: aniline (benzenamine).

concepts.name: Preparation of Amines â€“ Reduction of Nitro Compounds
concepts.explanation: Nitro compounds are reduced to amines using Hâ‚‚/Ni, Pd, or Pt (catalytic hydrogenation), or with metals in acidic medium (Fe/HCl, Sn/HCl, Zn/HCl). Example: Câ‚†Hâ‚…NOâ‚‚ + 3Hâ‚‚ â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…NHâ‚‚ + 2Hâ‚‚O (Ni). Fe/HCl is preferred for aromatic nitro compounds.
concepts.formula_reaction: RNOâ‚‚ + 3Hâ‚‚ â†’ RNHâ‚‚ + 2Hâ‚‚O (catalyst)

concepts.name: Preparation of Amines â€“ Ammonolysis of Alkyl Halides
concepts.explanation: Alkyl halides react with ethanolic ammonia in a sealed tube at 373 K to give primary amines. The reaction proceeds via Sâ‚™2 and can further react to give secondary, tertiary amines, and quaternary ammonium salts. Example: CHâ‚ƒBr + NHâ‚ƒ â†’ CHâ‚ƒNHâ‚‚ + HBr.
concepts.formula_reaction: R-X + NHâ‚ƒ â†’ RNHâ‚‚ + HX (excess NHâ‚ƒ)

concepts.name: Preparation of Amines â€“ Reduction of Nitriles
concepts.explanation: Nitriles (RCN) are reduced to primary amines (RCHâ‚‚NHâ‚‚) using Hâ‚‚/Ni (or Pd/Pt), LiAlHâ‚„, or Na/Câ‚‚Hâ‚…OH. Example: CHâ‚ƒCN + 2Hâ‚‚ â†’ CHâ‚ƒCHâ‚‚NHâ‚‚ (Ni). This increases the carbon chain by one atom.
concepts.formula_reaction: RCN + 2Hâ‚‚ â†’ RCHâ‚‚NHâ‚‚ (catalyst)

concepts.name: Preparation of Amines â€“ Reduction of Amides
concepts.explanation: Amides are reduced to primary amines using LiAlHâ‚„. Example: CHâ‚ƒCONHâ‚‚ + LiAlHâ‚„ â†’ CHâ‚ƒCHâ‚‚NHâ‚‚. The carbonyl group is reduced to CHâ‚‚.
concepts.formula_reaction: RCONHâ‚‚ + LiAlHâ‚„ â†’ RCHâ‚‚NHâ‚‚

concepts.name: Preparation of Amines â€“ Gabriel Phthalimide Synthesis
concepts.explanation: Phthalimide is treated with KOH to form potassium phthalimide, which reacts with alkyl halides to give N-alkylphthalimide. Hydrolysis with NaOH or hydrazine gives primary amines. Aromatic primary amines cannot be prepared by this method. Example: phthalimide â†’ potassium phthalimide â†’ N-ethylphthalimide â†’ ethylamine.
concepts.formula_reaction: Phthalimide + RX â†’ N-alkylphthalimide â†’ RNHâ‚‚ (hydrolysis)

concepts.name: Hofmann Bromamide Degradation Reaction
concepts.explanation: Primary amines are prepared by treating amides with bromine and NaOH (or KOH). The amine formed has one carbon less than the amide (migration of alkyl/aryl group from carbonyl carbon to nitrogen). Example: CHâ‚ƒCONHâ‚‚ + Brâ‚‚ + 4NaOH â†’ CHâ‚ƒNHâ‚‚ + 2NaBr + Naâ‚‚COâ‚ƒ + 2Hâ‚‚O.
concepts.formula_reaction: RCONHâ‚‚ + Brâ‚‚ + 4NaOH â†’ RNHâ‚‚ + 2NaBr + Naâ‚‚COâ‚ƒ + 2Hâ‚‚O

concepts.name: Physical Properties of Amines
concepts.explanation: Lower amines are gases with fishy odour. Boiling points: primary > secondary > tertiary (due to H-bonding). Solubility in water decreases with increasing molecular mass. Amines are soluble in organic solvents. Primary and secondary amines form intermolecular H-bonds; tertiary amines do not.

concepts.name: Basic Character of Amines
concepts.explanation: Amines are basic due to the unshared electron pair on nitrogen. They react with acids to form salts, which are soluble in water but insoluble in organic solvents. Basicity: aliphatic amines > ammonia > aromatic amines. Kb and pKb values determine basic strength (smaller pKb = stronger base).
concepts.formula_reaction: RNHâ‚‚ + Hâ‚‚O â‡Œ RNHâ‚ƒâپ؛ + OHâپ»; pKb = -log Kb

concepts.name: Basicity of Aliphatic Amines â€“ Factors
concepts.explanation: Aliphatic amines are stronger bases than ammonia due to the +I effect of alkyl groups. Order in gas phase: 3آ° > 2آ° > 1آ° > NHâ‚ƒ. In aqueous phase, solvation and steric effects influence the order: (Câ‚‚Hâ‚…)â‚‚NH > (Câ‚‚Hâ‚…)â‚ƒN > Câ‚‚Hâ‚…NHâ‚‚ > NHâ‚ƒ; (CHâ‚ƒ)â‚‚NH > CHâ‚ƒNHâ‚‚ > (CHâ‚ƒ)â‚ƒN > NHâ‚ƒ.

concepts.name: Basicity of Aromatic Amines
concepts.explanation: Aromatic amines (e.g., aniline) are weaker bases than ammonia. The lone pair on nitrogen is delocalised into the benzene ring (resonance), making it less available for protonation. Aniline has five resonating structures; anilinium ion has only two. Electron-donating groups increase basicity; electron-withdrawing groups decrease it.

concepts.name: Alkylation of Amines
concepts.explanation: Amines react with alkyl halides to form secondary, tertiary amines, and quaternary ammonium salts. Example: CHâ‚ƒNHâ‚‚ + CHâ‚ƒI â†’ (CHâ‚ƒ)â‚‚NH â†’ (CHâ‚ƒ)â‚ƒN â†’ (CHâ‚ƒ)â‚„Nâپ؛Iâپ».
concepts.formula_reaction: RNHâ‚‚ + R'X â†’ RNHâ‚‚R' + Xâپ» â†’ further alkylation

concepts.name: Acylation of Amines
concepts.explanation: Primary and secondary amines react with acid chlorides, anhydrides, or esters to form amides (acylation). The reaction is carried out in presence of a base (pyridine) to neutralise HCl. Example: CHâ‚ƒNHâ‚‚ + CHâ‚ƒCOCl â†’ CHâ‚ƒNHCOCHâ‚ƒ + HCl.
concepts.formula_reaction: RNHâ‚‚ + R'COCl â†’ R'CONHR + HCl

concepts.name: Carbylamine Reaction (Isocyanide Test)
concepts.explanation: Primary amines (aliphatic and aromatic) on heating with CHClâ‚ƒ and alcoholic KOH give isocyanides (carbylamines) with a foul smell. Secondary and tertiary amines do not react. Used as a test for primary amines. Example: RNHâ‚‚ + CHClâ‚ƒ + 3KOH â†’ RNC + 3KCl + 3Hâ‚‚O.
concepts.formula_reaction: RNHâ‚‚ + CHClâ‚ƒ + 3KOH â†’ RNC + 3KCl + 3Hâ‚‚O (heat)

concepts.name: Reaction with Nitrous Acid â€“ Primary Aliphatic Amines
concepts.explanation: Primary aliphatic amines react with HNOâ‚‚ (NaNOâ‚‚ + HCl) to form aliphatic diazonium salts, which are unstable and decompose to give alcohols with nitrogen gas evolution. This quantitative evolution of Nâ‚‚ is used in estimation of amino acids. Example: RNHâ‚‚ + HNOâ‚‚ â†’ ROH + Nâ‚‚ + Hâ‚‚O.
concepts.formula_reaction: RNHâ‚‚ + HNOâ‚‚ â†’ ROH + Nâ‚‚ + Hâ‚‚O (aliphatic)

concepts.name: Reaction with Nitrous Acid â€“ Primary Aromatic Amines (Diazotisation)
concepts.explanation: Primary aromatic amines react with HNOâ‚‚ at 273-278 K to form stable diazonium salts (arenediazonium salts). Example: Câ‚†Hâ‚…NHâ‚‚ + NaNOâ‚‚ + 2HCl â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…Nâ‚‚âپ؛Clâپ» + NaCl + 2Hâ‚‚O. This is called diazotisation.
concepts.formula_reaction: ArNHâ‚‚ + NaNOâ‚‚ + 2HCl â†’ ArNâ‚‚âپ؛Clâپ» + NaCl + 2Hâ‚‚O (273-278K)

concepts.name: Reaction with Nitrous Acid â€“ Secondary and Tertiary Amines
concepts.explanation: Secondary amines react with HNOâ‚‚ to form N-nitrosamines (yellow oily liquids). Tertiary amines form soluble salts with HNOâ‚‚, which on warming regenerate the amine. These reactions help distinguish between primary, secondary, and tertiary amines.
concepts.formula_reaction: Râ‚‚NH + HNOâ‚‚ â†’ Râ‚‚N-NO + Hâ‚‚O (N-nitrosamine)

concepts.name: Hinsberg's Test (Benzenesulphonyl Chloride Test)
concepts.explanation: p-Toluenesulphonyl chloride (Hinsberg's reagent) reacts differently with primary, secondary, and tertiary amines. Primary amines give N-alkylsulphonamides (soluble in alkali). Secondary amines give N,N-dialkylsulphonamides (insoluble in alkali). Tertiary amines do not react. Used to distinguish and separate amines.

concepts.name: Electrophilic Substitution in Aniline
concepts.explanation: Aniline is highly activated and directs electrophiles to ortho and para positions. However, direct nitration gives tarry products and meta derivatives due to protonation. Acetylation protects the -NHâ‚‚ group, allowing controlled substitution followed by hydrolysis. Bromination: aniline + Brâ‚‚ water â†’ 2,4,6-tribromoaniline (white precipitate).

concepts.name: Diazonium Salts â€“ Diazotisation
concepts.explanation: Diazonium salts (ArNâ‚‚âپ؛Xâپ») are prepared by diazotisation of primary aromatic amines with NaNOâ‚‚ and HCl at 273-278 K. Benzenediazonium chloride is a colourless crystalline solid, stable in cold, but decomposes in the dry state.
concepts.formula_reaction: ArNHâ‚‚ + NaNOâ‚‚ + 2HCl â†’ ArNâ‚‚âپ؛Clâپ» + NaCl + 2Hâ‚‚O (273-278K)

concepts.name: Sandmeyer Reaction â€“ Replacement by Cl, Br, CN
concepts.explanation: Diazonium salts react with cuprous chloride (Cuâ‚‚Clâ‚‚/HCl) to give aryl chlorides, cuprous bromide (Cuâ‚‚Brâ‚‚/HBr) to give aryl bromides, and cuprous cyanide (CuCN/KCN) to give aryl cyanides. Nitrogen is evolved. Example: Câ‚†Hâ‚…Nâ‚‚âپ؛Clâپ» + CuCl â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…Cl + Nâ‚‚ + CuClâ‚‚.
concepts.formula_reaction: ArNâ‚‚âپ؛Xâپ» + CuX â†’ ArX + Nâ‚‚ + CuXâ‚‚ (X = Cl, Br, CN)

concepts.name: Gattermann Reaction
concepts.explanation: Diazonium salts react with copper powder in the presence of HCl or HBr to give aryl chlorides or bromides. The yield is lower than in the Sandmeyer reaction. Example: Câ‚†Hâ‚…Nâ‚‚âپ؛Clâپ» + Cu/HCl â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…Cl + Nâ‚‚.
concepts.formula_reaction: ArNâ‚‚âپ؛Clâپ» + Cu/HCl â†’ ArCl + Nâ‚‚

concepts.name: Replacement by Iodide (KI)
concepts.explanation: Diazonium salts react with KI to give aryl iodides without a catalyst. Example: Câ‚†Hâ‚…Nâ‚‚âپ؛Clâپ» + KI â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…I + Nâ‚‚ + KCl.
concepts.formula_reaction: ArNâ‚‚âپ؛Clâپ» + KI â†’ ArI + Nâ‚‚ + KCl

concepts.name: Replacement by Fluoride (Schiemann Reaction)
concepts.explanation: Benzenediazonium chloride reacts with HBFâ‚„ to form benzenediazonium fluoroborate, which on heating decomposes to aryl fluoride. Example: Câ‚†Hâ‚…Nâ‚‚âپ؛Clâپ» + HBFâ‚„ â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…Nâ‚‚âپ؛BFâ‚„âپ» â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…F + BFâ‚ƒ + Nâ‚‚.
concepts.formula_reaction: ArNâ‚‚âپ؛Clâپ» + HBFâ‚„ â†’ ArNâ‚‚âپ؛BFâ‚„âپ» â†’ ArF + BFâ‚ƒ + Nâ‚‚ (heat)

concepts.name: Replacement by Hydroxyl Group
concepts.explanation: Diazonium salts are hydrolysed to phenols on warming with water. Example: Câ‚†Hâ‚…Nâ‚‚âپ؛Clâپ» + Hâ‚‚O â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…OH + Nâ‚‚ + HCl.
concepts.formula_reaction: ArNâ‚‚âپ؛Clâپ» + Hâ‚‚O â†’ ArOH + Nâ‚‚ + HCl (warm)

concepts.name: Replacement by Hydrogen (Reductive Removal)
concepts.explanation: Diazonium salts are reduced by hypophosphorous acid (Hâ‚ƒPOâ‚‚) or ethanol to give arenes. Example: Câ‚†Hâ‚…Nâ‚‚âپ؛Clâپ» + Hâ‚ƒPOâ‚‚ + Hâ‚‚O â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚† + Nâ‚‚ + Hâ‚ƒPOâ‚ƒ + HCl.
concepts.formula_reaction: ArNâ‚‚âپ؛Clâپ» + Hâ‚ƒPOâ‚‚ + Hâ‚‚O â†’ ArH + Nâ‚‚ + Hâ‚ƒPOâ‚ƒ + HCl

concepts.name: Replacement by Nitro Group
concepts.explanation: Diazonium fluoroborate is heated with aqueous NaNOâ‚‚ in the presence of copper to give nitroarenes. Example: Câ‚†Hâ‚…Nâ‚‚âپ؛BFâ‚„âپ» + NaNOâ‚‚/Cu â†’ Câ‚†Hâ‚…NOâ‚‚ + Nâ‚‚.
concepts.formula_reaction: ArNâ‚‚âپ؛BFâ‚„âپ» + NaNOâ‚‚/Cu â†’ ArNOâ‚‚ + Nâ‚‚

concepts.name: Coupling Reactions â€“ Azo Dyes
concepts.explanation: Diazonium salts react with phenols or aromatic amines (coupling reaction) to form azo compounds (Ar-N=N-Ar') which are coloured and used as dyes. Example: benzenediazonium chloride + phenol (pH 8-10) â†’ p-hydroxyazobenzene (orange dye).
concepts.formula_reaction: ArNâ‚‚âپ؛Clâپ» + Ar'OH (or Ar'NHâ‚‚) â†’ Ar-N=N-Ar' + HCl

concepts.name: Importance of Diazonium Salts in Synthesis
concepts.explanation: Diazonium salts are versatile intermediates for introducing -F, -Cl, -Br, -I, -CN, -OH, -NOâ‚‚, and -H into aromatic rings. They allow the preparation of aryl halides and other substituted aromatic compounds that cannot be prepared by direct substitution.
